html:
Janus Group JSC (HOSE:TCO)

TCO is no longer a clean rice-export thesis, and the prior net-cash/special-dividend version should be discarded. The better thesis is a small, liquid-enough Vietnamese listed vehicle undergoing a control change, foreign-room unlock, and asset-platform reset. At VND 15,550, the market cap is only about VND 487 billion, or roughly USD 19 million, yet the new Janus strategy is being aimed at logistics M&A, a lithium battery project, a Da Nang mixed-use real-estate option, and broader infrastructure/energy partnerships.

The variant view is that this is not cheap on trailing earnings, but it may still be early if Janus can turn the HOSE listing into a funded holding company. TCO has doubled from the VND 8,000 area, but the current price is still only 9.4x the FY2026 profit target of VND 52 billion and about 1.26x book value using CafeF’s latest BVPS around VND 12,320. That is not obviously expensive for a successful platform reset, but it is very expensive for the old low-margin rice/logistics business.

The latest operating numbers are mixed. Q2 FY2026 revenue rebounded to VND 508 billion, but gross profit was only VND 9.3 billion and net profit to parent was VND 9.4 billion, per CafeF. Tin Nhanh Chung Khoan reported first-half FY2026 net profit of VND 12.6 billion, down 66% year over year and only 24% of plan, with finance costs and transport pressure still visible. So the market is not buying current earnings quality. It is buying the probability that the new board can inject assets, attract capital, and shift the valuation base.

The strongest concrete catalyst is the foreign ownership room. Janus officially lifted foreign room from 0% to 49% from April 20, 2026, and Phu Hung noted foreign trading reached 14-20% of TCO volume in some sessions after the change. For a small Vietnamese name, that matters. A company with a USD 19 million market cap, 31.3 million shares outstanding, and a newly opened foreign room can re-rate quickly if a credible strategic investor appears.

The second catalyst is the new strategic map. Janus announced eight cooperation documents in May, including VDB credit review for projects, Pacific Lines logistics, Fabric, Poh Group, Mobii Green Energy, Quang Phuc, training, and insurance partners. The company has also approved a plan to invest up to VND 2,000 billion for at least 70% of a Da Nang mixed-use project, M&A 65-90% of Pacific Lines, and invest around VND 200 billion into lithium-ion/LFP battery manufacturing. The battery economics are the upside hook: management has discussed potential early-stage annual net profit of roughly VND 120-150 billion from that project. If even half of that becomes attributable and recurring, today’s market cap stops looking stretched.

The bear case is that this is mostly narrative. The old chairman and CEO sold out of more than 38% of the company before the reset. H1 earnings are weak. The current balance sheet is not a fortress, and the planned Da Nang and logistics investments dwarf the market cap. A VND 487 billion equity cannot fund VND 2,000 billion of project exposure without partner capital, leverage, dilution, or asset transfers. The MOUs are not cash flow. The decisive test is whether Janus signs binding financing and project economics that minority shareholders can underwrite.

The social setup is also speculative. F247 retail discussion frames TCO as a potential “super stock” and even talks about multi-bagger outcomes, while skeptics correctly focus on missing capital, technology partners, and execution proof. That is the right sentiment read: narrative momentum is real, but the stock is now priced for evidence, not just press releases.

My base case is that Janus gets enough strategic validation to deserve a platform premium, but not full credit for the battery or Da Nang economics yet. FY2026 profit of VND 52 billion is achievable only with a strong H2, so I would value it at about 11-12x confirmed forward earnings plus a small option value for foreign-room and M&A execution.

Targets from the current VND 15,550 price:

Bear: VND 7,500, down 52%. H2 misses, strategic deals stay non-binding, financing is dilutive, and the stock falls back toward 0.6x book.

Base: VND 22,000, up 41%. Janus hits or comes close to the VND 52 billion FY2026 profit plan, closes one credible strategic transaction, and trades near 12x forward earnings with option value.

Bull: VND 42,000, up 170%. Battery/logistics execution becomes real, attributable earnings power moves toward VND 120-160 billion, foreign capital enters, and the market values Janus as a small holding-company growth platform rather than a rice/logistics remnant.
